meta.title: Drift — Nomad Super App
meta.subtitle: A design concept exploring what happens when city discovery, remote jobs, and IRL community are organised around one place — not three separate platforms.

data.headline: A city isn't a location. It's a decision.
data.body: When a digital nomad considers relocating, they're not asking 'where is Prague?' They're asking three questions simultaneously: Can I afford it? Is there work I can actually get paid for? Will there be people worth knowing when I get there? Right now, those three questions live on three different platforms that don't talk to each other. This concept explores what the design looks like when you structure the answer around the city — not the task — and what hard decisions that forces you to make about information hierarchy, social proof, and what a 'profile' for a place actually needs to contain.

data.label: worldwide in 2023 — up 131% since 2019
data.body: Source: MBO Partners State of Independence 2023. A generation of workers who move between cities has already arrived. The tooling to support how they actually make relocation decisions hasn't caught up — the research is still split across five apps, none of which know about the others.

before.label: How nomads research a city today
items: Nomad List — check livability score and cost of living
Remote job board (Remote.co, We Work Remotely) — scan for open roles
Meetup or Telegram group — find local nomad events and community
Twitter or Discord — ask if anyone's been there, what it's actually like
Google Maps — manually check cafés, coworking spaces, neighborhood feel

after.label: The design hypotheses
items: Score and cost matter more than description — qualification is numerical first, qualitative second
Payment type is a qualifying criterion; it should be visible before the click, not inside the listing
Event attendance — who's going — is the deciding signal before what the event is
Jobs and events stay on the city page — the city is the frame, not the task type
A save action should work identically across jobs and events — the intent is the same

items.name: The crypto-native nomad
items.type: Composite · based on app's evident target user
items.biography: Earns in USDC or ETH. Moves cities every two to three months — not backpacking, but not settling either. Evaluates relocations the way some people evaluate apartments: score first, vibe second. Finds community through Bitcoin meetups and Telegram nomad groups rather than LinkedIn or Eventbrite.
items.goal: Find a city with a livable cost-to-income ratio, an active crypto-friendly remote job market, and enough nomads already there that the first two weeks don't feel like starting from zero.
items.needs: Quantitative city signals up front — not buried under a city description. Crypto payment options visible before clicking into a job listing. Event social proof (who's going) visible before the event description (what it is).
items.frustrations: Opening listing after listing to find one that pays in crypto. City research spread across four apps that don't share context. Showing up to a new city and discovering the nomad scene existed two weeks ago — and the next meetup is in a month.
items.why_use: Opens City Profile → checks Score and Cost before reading a word of description. Scans Job 20 count on the tab to gauge market depth before opening any listings. Checks Event 3 tab to see what's happening in the next two weeks. The numbers on the tabs are the entire first pass — text comes second. If the numbers pass threshold, Prague is shortlisted before a single listing is opened. If they don't, the city is skipped.

data.text: The three apps aren't the problem. They're the symptom. Jobs, city research, and community discovery are organised by task type — when for a nomad, they're all the same question: 'Should I move to Prague?'
data.attribution: — hypothesis that shaped every layout decision

data.title: Each screen bets on what matters first.

items.title: Score first, description later.
items.body: Score (9.2) and Cost ($3,200/mo) appear before any city description text. The hypothesis: nomads make city decisions quantitatively first, qualitatively second. The description is confirmation, not discovery. The tab bar (Event 3 · Job 20) makes the city feel active — not a static wiki entry.

items.title: Four currencies. One surface.
items.body: Payment options (USD · Euro · BTC · ETH) appear as equal pill badges on the card — not inside the detail view. For a freelancer whose income is already in crypto, this is a qualifying signal that needs to be visible before the click. Opening listing after listing to find one that pays in ETH is friction that compounds.

items.title: Who's going before what it is.
items.body: 'Nomad joined' (99+ avatars) appears before 'About the event'. The decision to attend an IRL event is social before it's informational. Seeing your crowd there answers the real question — 'will I know anyone?' — faster than any event description can.

data.headline: What the layout is arguing — and what it gave up.

items.title: Payment type on the card, not inside the listing
items.problem: A crypto-earning freelancer treats payment currency as a qualifying criterion — not a detail. Burying BTC/ETH inside the job detail means opening listing after listing to find one that pays in the right currency.

options.text: Payment shown in detail view only — standard card pattern, preserves readability and card density.
Currency pills (USD · EUR · BTC · ETH) visible on the job card surface, before any click.

items.cost: Four currency pills consume card space that could show two more lines of role description. The bet: payment type is a qualifying criterion for this user — so it ranks above requirements text.

items.title: Social proof above the event description
items.problem: Nomads don't primarily attend events for the content — they attend to meet people. A well-described event with zero nomads is less compelling than a vague one with 99 already going.

options.text: Standard event layout — name and description first, attendee list anchored at the bottom.
'99+ nomads joined' count appears high on the card, above the event name and description.

items.cost: A newcomer unfamiliar with the event has to scroll past the social proof signal before understanding what they're showing up to. The social proof may feel like noise before the context exists.

items.title: City page as a live hub, not a static profile
items.problem: Jobs and events aren't useful in the abstract — they're useful because they're in Prague. Keeping them on separate platforms strips the location context that makes them relevant to a relocation decision.

options.text: City page is static — score, cost, description, map. Jobs and events remain on their own platforms.
City page becomes a hub with tabs for dynamic content: Feed · Events · Jobs, all scoped to that city.

items.cost: Tabs require users to discover they exist. Count badges (Event 3 · Job 20) carry the entire signalling load — without them, the tabs read as empty navigation and the hub feels hollow.

items.title: One bookmark action across jobs and events
items.problem: A nomad building a Prague shortlist wants to save a job and an event, but these live in different apps with different save actions. The intent is identical; the surfaces are fragmented.

options.text: Type-specific save actions — RSVP for events, Save for jobs, different UI per content type.
Single bookmark icon, visually identical across both job cards and event cards.

items.cost: On the event screen, the bookmark now competes with 'Interest to join' — two save-like actions with overlapping intent. The hierarchy problem was noted but not resolved in this sprint.

data.title: Qualify fast. Go deep only when it passes.
data.description: A nomad's relocation decision is a single session — not three separate app sessions. Each screen earns the right to the next: the score is permission to open the Job tab. The job depth is permission to check whether the community already exists. If Prague fails at step one, none of the rest gets read.
steps: Open a job listing — verify crypto payment (ETH accepted)
Return to city — check Event tab
See 'Run For Hal Prague' — 99+ nomads joined

data.headline: What this sprint explored — and what it didn't.
data.body: Drift is a concept, not a shipped product. The sprint explored one hypothesis: organising city discovery, jobs, and community around a place — rather than a task type — forces specific decisions about information hierarchy and social proof ordering. The payment-type-on-card, social-proof-above-description, and count-badge-on-tab decisions all follow from that one reframing. What the concept does not validate: whether the crypto-native nomad audience is large enough to build for, whether a tab-heavy city hub creates navigation debt, or whether a unified bookmark across content types produces useful shortlists. Those remain open questions that would require user testing to resolve.

data.text: Nomads qualify cities with signals, not stories. Score before description. Social proof before event details. The ordering isn't aesthetic — it's the entire design argument, visible in three screens.
